1.synthesis: After Joshua’s death, the tribes ask, “Who shall go up first… to fight against the Canaanites?” and the LORD answers, “Judah shall go up” (vv.1–2). Judah, with Simeon, wins victories — capturing Adoni-bezek, taking Jerusalem and Hebron, and Caleb’s nephew Othniel seizing Kiriath-sepher to win Achsah (vv.4–15). Yet already cracks appear: “the people of Benjamin did not drive out the Jebusites” (v.21). The integrative thread is the bright but fading dawn of the conquest: where Israel seeks the LORD and acts in faith, victory follows, but the chapter’s repeated “did not drive out” signals the incomplete obedience that will doom the nation to the cycle of decline the book records.
1.historical_context: Judges opens “after the death of Joshua” (v.1), in the unsettled period before the monarchy. Judah’s leadership (v.2) anticipates its later royal primacy (Genesis 49:10). The Achsah episode (vv.12–15) — a daughter boldly asking for springs of water — preserves a glimpse of faithful initiative. The taking of Jerusalem (v.8) was incomplete, as the Jebusites remained until David (2 Samuel 5:6–7).
1.literary_structure: This opening conquest report (vv.1–21) contrasts sharply with the rest of the chapter and book: it begins with inquiry of the LORD and victory, but the refrain “did not drive out” (v.21, recurring through vv.27–33) sets up the downward spiral. The placement of victories first, then failures, mirrors the book’s movement from a faithful start to deepening compromise.
1.theological_application: Beginning with inquiry of the LORD and acting in faith brings victory (v.2; Proverbs 3:6), but partial obedience sows the seeds of future ruin. The chapter teaches that the Christian life, like the conquest, must be pursued to completion; tolerated pockets of unconquered sin become snares (Hebrews 12:1). Achsah’s bold asking for the springs models the faith that seeks the fullness of God’s promised blessing rather than settling for less (Matthew 7:7). The contrast within the chapter — victory where God is sought, decline where obedience falters — sets the pattern for the whole Christian life: what we leave un-mortified does not stay neutral but festers into a snare (Romans 8:13). Achsah’s bold request for the springs (Judges 1:15) models a faith unsatisfied with half-measures, pressing on to claim the fullness of God’s promised blessing rather than settling short of it (2 Corinthians 1:20).
1.christology: Judah goes up first (v.2), the tribe from which the Lion of Judah, the true King and Conqueror, would come (Revelation 5:5). Where these judges and tribes only partially conquered, Christ wins a complete and final victory, leaving no enemy undefeated (1 Corinthians 15:25). The incomplete conquest that dooms Israel to the cycle of sin magnifies the need for the greater Joshua-King who alone secures the rest his people could not win for themselves (Hebrews 4:8–10).

22.synthesis: The house of Joseph goes up against Bethel, “and the LORD was with them” (v.22). Spies find a man coming out of the city, and on his showing them the entrance they take the city, sparing him and his family, who go on to found a city named Luz (vv.23–26). The integrative thread is victory by the LORD’s presence, joined to a Rahab-like sparing of a helpful informant: the brief account shows that where “the LORD was with them” the city falls, and that even in conquest there is room for mercy to those who aid God’s people — though the spared man’s founding of another Luz hints at compromise lingering on.
22.historical_context: Bethel (“house of God”), formerly Luz (v.23), was significant from the patriarchs (Genesis 28:19). The sparing of the helpful man and his family (vv.24–25) parallels the rescue of Rahab at Jericho (Joshua 6:25), reflecting the practice of clemency toward those who assisted Israel. His founding a new Luz in Hittite territory (v.26) shows Canaanite life persisting alongside Israel.
22.literary_structure: This short episode (vv.22–26) stands between Judah’s successes and the catalog of failures that follows, with the key phrase “the LORD was with them” (v.22) marking the source of victory. It echoes the Jericho-Rahab pattern, weaving the theme of mercy to the outsider into the conquest narrative.
22.theological_application: “The LORD was with them” (v.22) is the secret of every true victory; success follows the presence of God, not merely human strategy (Psalm 127:1). The sparing of the helpful Canaanite shows that God’s mercy reaches those who aid his people and turn toward them, a foretaste of grace to the nations. Yet the lingering of Canaanite settlements warns that incomplete devotion leaves remnants that may later entangle (Numbers 33:55). The episode quietly teaches that all genuine success traces to the presence of God, not human cleverness (v.22; Psalm 127:1), and that his mercy reaches outsiders who turn toward his people. Yet the spared man’s founding of a new Luz hints that compromise unaddressed simply relocates (v.26), a reminder that tolerated remnants of the old life have a way of perpetuating themselves if not decisively dealt with (Hebrews 12:1).
22.christology: The sparing of the informant and his household, like Rahab’s rescue, foreshadows the gospel grace that saves outsiders who align themselves with God’s people and his purposes (Joshua 6:25; Matthew 1:5). The presence of the LORD that secured the victory (v.22) is fulfilled in Immanuel, “God with us” (Matthew 1:23), whose abiding presence guarantees his people’s triumph and gathers the nations to himself (Matthew 28:20).

27.pericope_label: Failure to Drive Out the Canaanites
27.synthesis: The chapter turns dark with a litany of failure: Manasseh, Ephraim, Zebulun, Asher, Naphtali, and Dan each “did not drive out” the inhabitants, but instead let the Canaanites dwell among them, sometimes putting them to forced labor rather than removing them (vv.27–33). The integrative thread is the comprehensive incompleteness of the conquest: tribe after tribe settles for coexistence with the Canaanites, preferring economic advantage (forced labor) to full obedience, and this compromise — recorded with relentless repetition — is the root cause of the apostasy and oppression that the rest of Judges will chronicle.
27.historical_context: The repeated “did not drive out” (vv.27–33) catalogs the partial conquest that left Canaanite enclaves throughout the land. Reducing Canaanites to “forced labor” (v.28, v.30) instead of removing them prioritized economic gain over obedience to God’s command (Deuteronomy 7:2). These surviving peoples and their gods became exactly the snare God had warned of (Judges 2:3).
27.literary_structure: The drumbeat repetition of “did not drive out” (v.27, v.29, v.30, etc.) is a deliberate literary device hammering home the national failure. The descent from Judah’s victories to Dan’s near-defeat (v.34) traces a downward gradient, the geography of failure preparing for the theological verdict of chapter 2.
27.theological_application: Compromise with sin for the sake of convenience or gain is the seedbed of spiritual ruin (v.28; Romans 6:12–13). What we tolerate rather than mortify will, in time, dominate us (Galatians 5:9). The tribes’ preference for forced labor over obedience warns that valuing earthly advantage above God’s commands leaves snares that entangle; the believer must put sin to death rather than make peace with it (Colossians 3:5). The repeated “did not drive out” (vv.27–33) is the human seed of the whole tragic cycle that follows: what is tolerated for convenience or gain becomes the master that enslaves (Galatians 5:9). The believer is warned to put sin to death rather than negotiate a profitable coexistence with it, for the Canaanite reduced to forced labor today becomes the snare and the idol of tomorrow (Colossians 3:5).
27.christology: Israel’s failure to drive out the enemy magnifies the completeness of Christ’s conquest, who came to destroy the works of the devil (1 John 3:8) and leaves no foe in uneasy coexistence but triumphs decisively (Colossians 2:15). Where the tribes settled for compromise, Christ sanctifies his people wholly (1 Thessalonians 5:23) and will at last present a kingdom with every enemy under his feet (1 Corinthians 15:25–26), the full obedience Israel never achieved accomplished in him. Israel’s repeated failure to drive out the enemy (v.27) throws into relief the completeness of Christ’s conquest, who tolerates no truce with the powers of darkness but triumphs over them openly (Colossians 2:15). Where the tribes settled for coexistence and forced labor, the greater Joshua will put every enemy under his feet (1 Corinthians 15:25) and sanctify his people wholly, finishing the obedience Israel left undone (Philippians 1:6).
